posts.title: Why Your Annual Physical Is the Most Important Appointment You're Skipping
posts.author: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD
posts.excerpt: Most people only see a doctor when something is already wrong. Here's why a yearly wellness visit — when you feel perfectly fine — is the most valuable thing you can do for your long-term health.
posts.body:
## The Appointment No One Prioritizes

If you're like most adults, you schedule a dentist appointment every six months without much thought. But when it comes to seeing your primary care physician for a full wellness exam — when nothing is actively wrong — it keeps getting pushed to next month, then next quarter, then next year.

We understand. Life is busy. And it's hard to feel urgency about something that doesn't hurt.

But here's what that mindset misses: the entire point of an annual physical is to catch problems **before** they hurt — before they show symptoms at all.

## What Actually Happens at a Wellness Visit

A comprehensive annual physical at Meridian Health covers far more than listening to your heart and reflexes. Your physician will:

- **Review your bloodwork** — cholesterol, blood sugar, thyroid, kidney function, vitamin levels, and more
- **Screen for age- and risk-appropriate conditions** — colon cancer, skin changes, blood pressure trends, bone density
- **Update your medication list** and flag any interactions or outdated prescriptions
- **Talk through your mental health** — anxiety, sleep quality, and stress are medical issues, not personal failings
- **Revisit your family history** and adjust your screening schedule based on evolving risk
- **Set goals with you** — not at you — around nutrition, exercise, sleep, and stress management

This visit is your health baseline. Every data point collected becomes part of a longitudinal picture that helps your physician notice when something shifts — even subtly.

## The Conditions Most Commonly Caught at Wellness Visits

These are conditions that rarely produce symptoms until they're advanced — which is exactly why proactive screening matters so much:

- **Hypertension (high blood pressure)** — affects 1 in 3 adults; almost always silent until organ damage occurs
- **Pre-diabetes and Type 2 diabetes** — caught early, both are reversible with lifestyle change
- **Elevated cholesterol** — no symptoms, but a major driver of heart attack and stroke risk
- **Thyroid dysfunction** — fatigue and weight changes are often misattributed to stress or aging
- **Colorectal cancer** — detected early, survival rates exceed 90%; detected late, outcomes are poor
- **Skin cancer** — a full skin check during your physical can identify lesions you'd never notice yourself

None of these conditions wait for a convenient time. But they do respond well to early action.

## "But I Feel Fine"

This is the most common reason people delay their annual physical — and the most important reason not to.

Feeling fine is a lagging indicator of health. Many of the most serious conditions are asymptomatic for years. By the time you feel them, treatment is more complex, more expensive, and sometimes less effective.

Wellness visits aren't about finding problems. They're about understanding your full health picture — so you and your doctor can make smart, proactive decisions together.

## How Often Should You Go?

For most adults under 50 in good health, once a year is appropriate. If you have chronic conditions, a family history of serious illness, or are managing active health issues, your physician may recommend more frequent check-ins.

The important thing isn't the exact frequency — it's consistency. A relationship with a primary care physician who knows your history over years is one of the most powerful health resources you can have.

## Ready to Schedule?

Meridian Health is currently accepting new patients, and we hold same-day slots for urgent needs alongside dedicated time for full wellness exams. If it's been more than a year since your last physical — or you've never established care with a primary care doctor — this is a good moment to change that.

[Book your annual physical →](/contact.html)

posts.title: Telehealth vs. In-Person Visits: How to Know Which Is Right for You
posts.author: Dr. Marcus Webb, MD
posts.excerpt: Telehealth is a genuine convenience for the right situations — but it isn't the right fit for every concern. Here's a practical guide to help you decide which type of visit to book.
posts.body:
## A New Default That Isn't Always the Best Default

Over the past few years, telehealth has become the default for many people with minor health concerns. And for good reason — it's fast, convenient, and eliminates the commute. But convenience isn't the only factor in good medicine. Knowing when to book a video call versus when to come in can meaningfully affect the quality of your care.

Here's a practical breakdown.

## When Telehealth Works Well

Telehealth is genuinely excellent for a specific set of situations:

**Straightforward acute concerns**
- Cold and flu symptoms (assessment, guidance, prescriptions if appropriate)
- Sinus infections or uncomplicated UTIs
- Mild rashes that photograph clearly
- Minor eye irritation or allergies
- Stomach bugs without red flags

**Ongoing management**
- Medication refills and routine prescription reviews
- Follow-up on recent lab results
- Checking in on a chronic condition that's been stable
- Mental health check-ins between therapy sessions

**Convenience situations**
- You're traveling or temporarily away from home
- Your schedule makes getting to the office genuinely difficult
- You want a quick question answered by your own doctor rather than a stranger at urgent care

## When You Should Come In

Some things cannot be adequately assessed over video — no matter how good the technology is. Come in person for:

**Physical examination needs**
- Chest pain, shortness of breath, or heart palpitations
- Abdominal pain that's new or worsening
- Joint pain, swelling, or suspected injury
- Lumps, bumps, or lesions that need to be felt, not just seen
- Any symptom that's been present for more than a week without improvement

**Diagnostic needs**
- Situations where bloodwork, urine testing, EKG, or imaging is likely
- Concerns that might require an on-site procedure (wound care, injections, biopsies)
- Pre-surgical clearances or insurance-required physicals

**Nuance-heavy conversations**
- Discussing a new significant diagnosis
- Addressing mental health concerns that feel serious or crisis-adjacent
- Any appointment where you want to be *in the room* with your doctor — and that instinct is worth trusting

## A Useful Rule of Thumb

If your concern involves something your doctor needs to **touch, hear through a stethoscope, or observe in person** — come in. If your concern is primarily informational, conversational, or involves a condition your doctor already knows well, telehealth is often the right call.

When in doubt, our team can help you decide during the booking process. We'd rather you come in unnecessarily than miss something because a visit was too convenient to question.

## Telehealth at Meridian Health

All Meridian Health telehealth visits are conducted securely, connect you directly to your own physician (not a stranger), and are followed up with a summary in your patient portal. We treat them with the same thoroughness as an in-person appointment — because the relationship, not the channel, is what matters.

[Schedule a telehealth or in-person visit →](/contact.html)

posts.title: Understanding Your Blood Pressure Numbers: What They Mean and What to Do
posts.author: Dr. Marcus Webb, MD
posts.excerpt: High blood pressure affects nearly 1 in 3 adults — and most don't know they have it. Here's a plain-language guide to what your numbers actually mean and how to take control of them.
posts.body:
## The Silent Risk Most Adults Are Carrying

Blood pressure is one of those numbers that feels abstract — two digits on a cuff, measured for thirty seconds, then forgotten. But those numbers are telling a continuous story about the health of your heart, arteries, kidneys, and brain.

Hypertension (chronically elevated blood pressure) affects roughly 47% of American adults. Of those, nearly 1 in 5 don't know they have it — because it produces no symptoms until significant damage has already occurred.

Let's decode the numbers.

## Reading the Cuff: Systolic and Diastolic

Blood pressure is expressed as two numbers: **120/80**, for example.

- The **top number (systolic)** measures the pressure in your arteries when your heart beats and pumps blood outward.
- The **bottom number (diastolic)** measures the pressure when your heart rests between beats.

Both numbers matter, and both are tracked over time.

## What the Ranges Mean

| Category | Systolic | | Diastolic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal | Less than 120 | and | Less than 80 |
| Elevated | 120–129 | and | Less than 80 |
| High — Stage 1 | 130–139 | or | 80–89 |
| High — Stage 2 | 140+ | or | 90+ |
| Crisis (seek care) | Higher than 180 | and/or | Higher than 120 |

A single high reading isn't a diagnosis. Blood pressure fluctuates based on stress, caffeine, exercise, hydration, and even the anxiety of being in a doctor's office ("white coat hypertension"). Your physician looks for a consistent pattern across multiple readings.

## Why Hypertension Is Dangerous

Elevated pressure forces your heart to work harder with every beat. Over years, this damages:

- **Artery walls** — making them stiffer and more prone to plaque buildup
- **The heart** — increasing the risk of heart attack, heart failure, and irregular rhythm
- **The kidneys** — a leading cause of chronic kidney disease
- **The brain** — significantly raising stroke risk
- **The eyes** — hypertensive retinopathy can impair vision

None of this produces pain or obvious symptoms while it's happening. That's what makes regular monitoring so critical.

## What You Can Do

**Lifestyle changes that genuinely move the number:**

- **Reduce sodium** — aim for less than 2,300mg per day; most Americans consume 3,400mg
- **Move regularly** — 30 minutes of moderate activity most days can lower systolic pressure by 5–8 points
- **Limit alcohol** — more than 1–2 drinks per day raises blood pressure measurably
- **Manage stress** — chronic stress keeps cortisol elevated, which keeps blood pressure elevated
- **Maintain a healthy weight** — each 10-pound loss can reduce systolic pressure by 4–5 points
- **Quit smoking** — nicotine spikes blood pressure immediately and damages vessel walls over time

For many patients with Stage 1 hypertension, these changes are sufficient. For Stage 2 or patients with other cardiovascular risk factors, medication is often appropriate — and highly effective.

## When to Talk to Your Doctor

If you've had two or more readings above 130/80 — even at home with a personal cuff — it's worth a conversation. If you've had any reading above 160/100, don't wait for your next annual physical.

Meridian Health physicians take a collaborative approach to blood pressure management: we start with the full picture (labs, lifestyle, family history, stress load) before recommending any intervention. The goal is always the same — a number that protects your long-term health and fits your actual life.

[Schedule a blood pressure check or full cardiovascular review →](/contact.html)

posts.title: What Every Woman Should Know About Hormonal Health
posts.author: Dr. Priya Nair, MD
posts.excerpt: Hormonal health affects energy, mood, sleep, metabolism, and long-term disease risk — yet it's one of the most under-discussed topics in primary care. Here's a clear-eyed guide to what to ask your doctor.
posts.body:
## More Than a "Women's Issue"

When patients come to me with fatigue that won't lift, sleep that's never restorative, weight that's shifting despite no changes in diet, or moods that feel unpredictable — the first thing I want to understand is their hormonal picture.

Hormones are chemical messengers that regulate virtually everything: metabolism, sleep architecture, mood regulation, immune function, bone density, cardiovascular health, and cognitive sharpness. When they're in balance, you feel like yourself. When they're not, it can be hard to identify why — because the symptoms overlap with so many other things.

This guide is designed to give you a clearer vocabulary for these conversations with your physician.

## The Key Hormones in Women's Health

**Estrogen** — The primary female sex hormone, produced mainly in the ovaries. It regulates the menstrual cycle, supports bone density, influences cardiovascular health, affects mood via serotonin, and plays a significant role in skin and connective tissue integrity. Levels fluctuate across the menstrual cycle and decline significantly during perimenopause and menopause.

**Progesterone** — Works in counterbalance to estrogen. It supports uterine health, promotes sleep quality, and has a calming effect on the nervous system. Low progesterone relative to estrogen ("estrogen dominance") is common and can cause irregular cycles, PMS symptoms, and sleep disruption.

**Testosterone** — Yes, women produce testosterone too — in smaller amounts, but it matters. It influences libido, muscle mass, energy, and cognitive focus. Levels decline with age and can also be suppressed by hormonal contraceptives.

**Thyroid hormones (T3/T4)** — Not sex hormones, but profoundly connected to hormonal health. Thyroid dysfunction — particularly hypothyroidism — is significantly more common in women and can mimic or exacerbate hormonal imbalance symptoms.

**Cortisol** — The stress hormone. Chronically elevated cortisol suppresses other hormonal systems, disrupts sleep, increases abdominal fat storage, and accelerates hormonal aging. Managing stress isn't just a wellness platitude — it's a clinical intervention.

**Insulin** — Insulin resistance, increasingly common in younger women (especially those with PCOS), is a hormonal issue with cascading effects on weight, energy, inflammation, and long-term metabolic risk.

## Symptoms Worth Investigating

None of these symptoms should be dismissed as "just getting older" or "just stress":

- Persistent fatigue unresolved by sleep
- Night sweats or hot flashes (at any age, not just menopause)
- Irregular, heavy, or painful periods
- Worsening PMS or PMDD
- Hair thinning or loss
- Brain fog, memory lapses, concentration difficulty
- Unexplained weight gain — particularly abdominal
- Reduced libido
- Mood changes — anxiety, depression, or irritability disproportionate to circumstances
- Dry skin, joint aches, or changes in body composition

Any one of these alone might be unrelated to hormones. But if you're experiencing a cluster of these symptoms — especially if they've appeared or worsened together — a hormonal workup is warranted.

## What Hormonal Testing Involves

A basic hormonal panel for women typically includes:

- Estradiol (E2) and progesterone — ideally timed to your cycle
- Testosterone (total and free)
- TSH, Free T3, Free T4 (thyroid)
- DHEA-S (adrenal function marker)
- Fasting insulin and glucose
- Sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG)
- LH and FSH (especially relevant for fertility or perimenopause assessment)

This isn't a standard blood panel — you'll need to ask for it specifically, or see a physician (like those at Meridian Health) who proactively includes it in wellness assessments.

## Perimenopause: Earlier Than You Think

Perimenopause — the hormonal transition before menopause — can begin as early as the mid-30s, though most commonly in the 40s. It doesn't begin with hot flashes. It often begins with subtler changes: irregular cycles, worsening PMS, sleep disruption, or mood shifts that feel new.

Understanding where you are in this transition allows your physician to make much more targeted recommendations — whether that's lifestyle adjustments, nutritional support, or, when appropriate, hormone therapy.

## A Note on Hormone Therapy

Hormone therapy (HT) was significantly overstated as dangerous for decades following a misinterpreted 2002 study. The current evidence, when carefully reviewed, shows that hormone therapy initiated in the early perimenopause or menopause years provides meaningful cardiovascular, bone, and quality-of-life benefits for many women — with a risk profile that is acceptable for most.

This is a nuanced conversation that should be individualized. I'm not suggesting it's right for everyone. I am suggesting it shouldn't be dismissed reflexively — and that you deserve a physician willing to have the full conversation.

## What to Ask at Your Next Visit

Come prepared with:
- A timeline of when your symptoms started
- A description of your menstrual cycle, if relevant
- Your family history of hormonal conditions (PCOS, thyroid, early menopause)
- Any hormonal contraceptives you're currently using

The more context you bring, the more useful that conversation becomes.

[Schedule a women's health consultation with Dr. Nair →](/contact.html)

posts.title: How to Build a Real Relationship with Your Primary Care Doctor
posts.author: Dr. Sarah Chen, MD
posts.excerpt: The most powerful health resource most people have isn't a specialist, a supplement, or a wearable device. It's a primary care physician who knows them well. Here's how to build that relationship intentionally.
posts.body:
## The Most Undervalued Asset in Healthcare

Studies consistently show that patients with a long-term primary care relationship have better health outcomes, lower hospitalization rates, fewer emergency department visits, and lower overall healthcare costs — compared to patients who use urgent care, emergency rooms, or specialists as their primary point of contact.

That's not a small effect. It's among the most robust findings in health services research.

And yet many adults don't have a primary care physician at all, or have one they see so rarely that no real relationship exists.

This is worth changing — and it's more intentional than most people realize.

## What "Relationship" Actually Means in Medicine

A good doctor-patient relationship isn't about chemistry or warmth (though those matter). It's about **longitudinal knowledge** — the accumulated understanding that comes from seeing someone over time, across different health states, across different life circumstances.

When your doctor knows:
- Your baseline vital signs (so they notice when something shifts)
- Your family history and what risks it implies
- Your tendency to minimize symptoms (or amplify them)
- The stressors in your life that affect your health
- Your medication history and what's worked before
- Your values and preferences around treatment

...then every appointment becomes more efficient, more accurate, and more personalized. Diagnoses that would be missed in a one-off urgent care visit get caught. Patterns that develop slowly over years get noticed. Decisions get made with full context instead of the narrow window of a single encounter.

## How to Build This Relationship

**1. Show up even when you feel fine.**
The annual physical isn't just a screening event. It's an investment in a relationship. Every year you see your doctor when nothing is wrong, the baseline deepens and the trust grows.

**2. Be honest, especially about what's hard to say.**
Doctors aren't here to judge your alcohol intake, your diet, your mental health, or your lifestyle choices. We need accurate information to give you good care. The most useful patients are the honest ones.

**3. Bring your questions.**
Write them down before you come in. Patients who arrive with a written list get more from their appointments — because they don't forget what they wanted to ask, and because it signals to the physician that you're an engaged participant.

**4. Tell the full story.**
Don't just describe the symptom. Describe when it started, what makes it better or worse, what you've tried, and how much it's affecting your life. Context is everything.

**5. Follow up on things that don't resolve.**
If a concern isn't improving, don't assume it will eventually go away. Your doctor wants to know — and in most cases, a brief message through the patient portal is all it takes.

**6. Let your doctor know when life changes.**
A new stressful job, a divorce, a loss, a move — these aren't separate from your health. They affect your immune function, your sleep, your cardiovascular risk. Your doctor should know the context of your life.

## Finding the Right Fit

Not every physician is the right fit for every patient — and that's okay. What matters is finding someone whose communication style, thoroughness, and approach match what you need.

At Meridian Health, we encourage new patients to come in knowing that the first visit is as much about mutual evaluation as it is about a health assessment. If you don't feel heard after a few visits, say so — or explore whether one of our other physicians is a better match.

A relationship built on the right foundation will serve you better for decades than one you settled into by default.

## The Long Game

Good health isn't a single decision. It's a series of small decisions, made over years, with good information. A primary care physician who knows you well is the person best positioned to help you make those decisions.

That relationship is worth investing in — starting with your first appointment.

[Meet our providers and book a first visit →](/providers.html)

posts.title: 5 Preventive Screenings Adults Under 50 Often Skip (and Shouldn't)
posts.author: Dr. Priya Nair, MD
posts.excerpt: Preventive screenings aren't just for older adults. Several of the most important — and most commonly skipped — are recommended for people in their 20s, 30s, and 40s. Here's what you may be missing.
posts.body:
## The Myth of the "Too Young to Screen" Mindset

Preventive medicine isn't something you opt into at 50. Many of the most important screenings — the ones that identify conditions while they're still fully reversible — are recommended for adults in their 20s, 30s, and 40s. And many of the most impactful health interventions work precisely because they happen early.

Here are five screenings that adults under 50 consistently delay — and why that matters.

## 1. Blood Pressure (Starting at 18)

**Recommended frequency:** Every 1–2 years for normal readings; annually or more if elevated

Hypertension is sometimes called a disease of the 50s and 60s. It shouldn't be. Rates of high blood pressure in adults under 40 have increased significantly over the past two decades — driven by rising rates of obesity, sedentary behavior, chronic stress, and poor diet.

The measurement takes 30 seconds. The consequences of missing it — heart disease, stroke, kidney disease — take years to reverse, if they can be reversed at all.

If you haven't had your blood pressure checked in the past year, it's overdue.

## 2. Cholesterol Panel (Starting at 20)

**Recommended frequency:** Every 4–6 years for low-risk adults; more often if levels are elevated or family history is significant

Familial hypercholesterolemia — a genetic condition causing very high LDL cholesterol — affects approximately 1 in 250 adults and dramatically increases cardiovascular risk. Most people with it have no idea.

Even without a genetic component, elevated cholesterol in your 20s and 30s has a compounding effect over time that's significantly more damaging than the same elevation starting at 55. The artery plaques don't start forming at your first bad lab result. They've been building since adolescence.

A baseline lipid panel in your early 20s gives your doctor an important reference point — and catching elevated LDL at 28 is a very different clinical situation than catching it at 58.

## 3. Blood Glucose / Prediabetes Screening (Starting at 35, Earlier with Risk Factors)

**Recommended frequency:** Every 3 years from age 35; earlier if BMI ≥25, family history of diabetes, or sedentary lifestyle

Over 96 million Americans — roughly 1 in 3 — have prediabetes. Approximately 80% of them don't know it.

Prediabetes is not a guaranteed progression to Type 2 diabetes. Caught early, it is frequently reversible with lifestyle change. But the window for that intervention narrows with each year it goes undetected.

The test is a simple fasting blood glucose or HbA1c — both part of routine bloodwork at your annual physical. This is one of the clearest examples of a condition where timing is everything.

## 4. Skin Cancer Screening (Starting at 20)

**Recommended frequency:** Annually, more frequently if you have a history of significant sun exposure, fair skin, or atypical moles

Melanoma — the deadliest form of skin cancer — has been increasing in incidence among adults under 40, particularly women. UV exposure in your teens and 20s creates a cumulative risk that manifests in your 30s and beyond.

Full-body skin checks are fast, non-invasive, and often done as part of a comprehensive physical exam. The visual changes that indicate early melanoma are subtle — the kind of thing most people wouldn't notice on their own back, scalp, or behind their ears.

If it's been more than two years since anyone looked at your full skin surface, ask your physician at your next visit.

## 5. Mental Health Screening (Every Visit)

**Recommended frequency:** Annually at minimum — the USPSTF recommends screening all adults for depression and anxiety at every preventive visit

Depression affects approximately 21 million American adults. Anxiety disorders are the most common mental health conditions in the country. Neither is screened for consistently — in part because primary care visits are busy, and in part because patients underreport symptoms they don't associate with "medical" concerns.

Mental health screening doesn't require a psychologist. At Meridian Health, validated screening tools (PHQ-9 for depression, GAD-7 for anxiety) are part of our standard intake process. A positive screen leads to a conversation — not a prescription. But that conversation can open doors that would otherwise stay closed for years.

Mental health is health. Screening for it as routinely as blood pressure is not an indulgence — it's good medicine.

## The Bottom Line

None of these screenings are time-consuming, expensive, or unpleasant. Most are part of a standard annual physical. What they have in common is that they surface actionable information early — when interventions are simpler, less invasive, and more effective.

If you're under 50 and haven't established care with a primary care physician who's tracking these over time, this is a good moment to start.

[Schedule a preventive care visit →](/contact.html)
